one_line_takeaway: Random sparse activation-steering at generation time creates a self-attribution watermark recoverable purely from a model's own internal activations (even via untrained cosine similarity), beating perplexity baselines and KGW token watermarking on paraphrase robustness, but the scheme's security is admittedly fragile if the secret steering vector/layer leaks and it does not generalize across model architectures.
key_finding: Self-recognition (Table 1, XL-Sum, AUROC %): with the original prompt available, the activation-based LDA probe reaches near-ceiling performance (100 for Ministral-3-8B, 99.99 for Llama-3.1-8B, 99.96 for Llama-3.2-3B, 99.82 for Llama-3.2-1B), matching the perplexity baseline (99.71/99.19/99.43/97.07 respectively) in that setting. In the prompt-agnostic setting the activation method stays high (99.99/99.16/99.03/98.58, dropping by at most ~1 AUROC point) while the perplexity baseline collapses to near/below chance (32.33/47.86/47.49/52.27) -- for Ministral-3-8B the perplexity ordering is actually inverted, which the authors attribute to possible train/eval data overlap. Multi-model attribution (Table 2, F1 %, text-level): Ministral-3-8B 100 (ELI5) / 100 (Fresh News); Llama-3.1-8B 99.1/99.1; Llama-3.2-3B 95.5/88.3; Llama-3.2-1B 85.3/83.8 -- performance scales with model size, and ELI5 (QA, ostensibly lower-entropy) is counterintuitively easier to attribute than open-ended Fresh News generation. Scaling to more classes (Fig. 2) degrades performance gracefully but stays well above the random 1/N baseline up to 20 classes. Sparse (99.7%) steering vectors dominate dense ones on the quality-vs-detectability Pareto frontier (Fig. 4). Robustness (Fig. 3): the activation method is more robust to DIPPER-XXL paraphrasing than the KGW token-watermark baseline, especially in free-form generation, though paraphrasing the human-reference text too degrades the method's F1 (spoofing effect, detailed in Appendix C/Fig. 8). Training-free cosine-similarity attribution (Table 3, two-class): text-level accuracy 84.6% (original) / 77.8% (paraphrased) vs. the trained MLP's 99.1%/89.3% -- lower than the supervised MLP but far above chance, supporting the claim that the signal is geometrically 'physically encoded,' not a classifier artifact. A hard architectural boundary is reported (Appendix G, referenced in Discussion): detection stays strong within a model family but collapses to chance across architectures.
core_contribution: The paper (Ardoin, Schäfer, Wunder; FU Berlin, ICML 2026) shows two things that are the authors' own claims, not cited prior work: (1) LLMs implicitly encode a linearly-separable 'self-recognition' signal in their internal activations that lets a lightweight LDA probe (with Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage covariance regularization) distinguish an LLM's own generations from human text, even for short, low-entropy outputs such as 1-2 sentence news summaries, and even in a prompt-agnostic setting where only the generated text (not the prompt) is available to the detector. (2) They introduce a steering-based, activation-space watermarking/attribution method: at generation time a random, highly sparse (99.7% zero dimensions) vector v is added to the residual stream at a middle layer (Al(xi) <- Al(xi) + alpha*v, alpha=5), producing a model/instance-specific signature. At detection time the same text is re-run through the (unsteered) model, activations are extracted at the same layer, and the source steering vector is recovered either via a trained two-hidden-layer (width 32) MLP classifier or, more strikingly, via simple cosine similarity between the extracted activations and candidate steering vectors -- a training-free detector that the authors argue demonstrates the signal is 'physically encoded' in the generated sequence rather than a statistical artifact of the classifier.
threat_model: White-box detection/attribution: the detector has white-box access to the internal activations of the same target model M that generated the text, either with the original prompt (prompt-conditioned) or without it (prompt-agnostic), and performs a forward pass over the candidate text to extract activations. This is explicitly scoped as an attribution/watermarking threat model 'standard in the watermarking literature' (per the Impact Statement), not an adversarial-attack threat model against a defender; the paper does not claim a black-box attacker setting. Robustness is evaluated against one text-transformation attack: DIPPER-XXL paraphrasing (lexical diversity 60, reordering 20), applied to the AI-generated text to try to wash out the watermark. The authors explicitly flag as an unaddressed threat that if an attacker recovers the secret steering vector and target layer, the watermark can be trivially spoofed (Discussion, 'Robustness and Secrecy'), and that robustness to recursive translation, adversarial perturbations, text degradation, or format changes 'remains to be characterized.'
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) detection requires white-box access and a forward pass over the text, costing ~21% of the compute/time needed to autoregressively generate the same text on an RTX A5500 (non-zero detection overhead despite zero generation-time cost). (2) The external quality-classifier-deberta evaluator has known biases (e.g., penalizing markdown formatting regardless of content), so it may mask small real quality degradations from steering. (3) Robustness was tested only against DIPPER-XXL paraphrasing; recursive translation, adversarial perturbation, text degradation, and format changes are explicitly left uncharacterized. (4) The scheme's security depends on secrecy of the steering vector and target layer -- if an attacker recovers these, spoofing is 'trivial' (their word); the authors propose (but do not implement) a rotating/pseudo-random steering schedule as a mitigation for future work. (5) Detection does not generalize across model architectures/families (chance-level cross-architecture performance). (6) Multi-bit/combinatorial watermarking via superimposed sparse vectors is proposed only theoretically and not empirically validated for interference effects. (7) The semantic/interpretability consequences of injecting a random steering vector are not fully characterized -- the authors state they could not identify obvious output artifacts but acknowledge a subtle distributional shift is inevitable and call for further interpretability work (e.g., SAE-based analysis) before deployment.

datasets_used: XL-Sum (English BBC subset, articles up to 2048 characters, 8192 articles) - self-recognition
ELI5 (filtered to remove 'Edit'/'URL' mention questions) - multi-model attribution, long-form QA
Fresh News (custom-curated via The Guardian API, articles published Nov 1 2025-Jan 5 2026, first six words used as prompt) - multi-model attribution, open-ended high-entropy generation
MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020) - used to check for steering-induced capability degradation
quantitative_results: Self-recognition AUROC with prompt, activation method: Ministral-3-8B 100, Llama-3.1-8B 99.99, Llama-3.2-3B 99.96, Llama-3.2-1B 99.82 (Table 1)
Self-recognition AUROC without prompt, activation method: Ministral-3-8B 99.99, Llama-3.1-8B 99.16, Llama-3.2-3B 99.03, Llama-3.2-1B 98.58 vs. perplexity baseline 32.33/47.86/47.49/52.27 (Table 1)
Multi-model attribution F1 (text-level): Ministral-3-8B 100/100, Llama-3.1-8B 99.1/99.1, Llama-3.2-3B 95.5/88.3, Llama-3.2-1B 85.3/83.8 on ELI5/Fresh News (Table 2)
Cosine-similarity (training-free) two-class attribution accuracy: text-level 84.6% original / 77.8% paraphrased vs. MLP 99.1%/89.3% (Table 3)
Detection compute overhead: ~21% of generation time on an RTX A5500 (Limitations)
Steering vector sparsity used: 99.7% zero dimensions, amplification coefficient alpha=5, applied at a middle layer (Section 3.2 / Appendix A)
baselines_compared: Perplexity-based detection (single-model, no auxiliary features) - self-recognition baseline (Table 1)
KGW watermark (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023, green-list token-probability watermarking) - paraphrase-robustness baseline (Fig. 3, Appendix Fig. 8)
Trained MLP classifier vs. simple cosine-similarity attribution - internal ablation of detection method (Table 3)
